tips: Many MCQ distractors use correct psychology terms in the wrong context — read carefully
For FRQs, always define the term AND apply it to the scenario for full credit
Research methods questions appear on ~25% of the exam — know IVs, DVs, confounds, and correlation vs. causation
All 5 units carry equal weight (~15 MCQs each) — do not neglect any unit

units.name: Biological Bases of Behavior
key_topics: Neuron anatomy and the action potential (all-or-none principle)
Neurotransmitters: dopamine, serotonin, acetylcholine, GABA, norepinephrine, glutamate, endorphins
CNS vs. PNS; somatic vs. autonomic; sympathetic vs. parasympathetic
Brain structures: brainstem (medulla, pons), cerebellum, limbic system (amygdala, hippocampus, hypothalamus), thalamus
Cerebral cortex lobes and specialized areas (Broca's, Wernicke's, motor cortex, somatosensory cortex)
Lateralization, corpus callosum, and split-brain research (Sperry)
Neuroplasticity and brain research methods (EEG, fMRI, PET, lesion studies)
Behavioral genetics: twin/adoption studies, heritability, gene-environment interaction
Sleep stages (NREM 1-3, REM), circadian rhythms, sleep disorders
Psychoactive drugs: depressants, stimulants, hallucinogens, opiates; tolerance and dependence
learning_objectives: Explain how neurotransmitters and neural structures influence behavior and mental processes
Describe the organization of the nervous system and each division's role
Identify brain regions by function and explain consequences of damage to specific areas
Explain how genetics and evolution contribute to psychological traits
Describe how sleep and drug use affect biological processes
key_researchers: Ronald Melzack & Patrick Wall — Gate Control Theory of pain
common_question_types: Case study: patient has brain damage — identify the affected region from the described symptom
Match a neurotransmitter to its function or associated disorder
Distinguish sympathetic vs. parasympathetic response in a scenario
Identify the research method (fMRI, lesion study, etc.) best suited to a described question
exam_tips: FRQ responses must name a specific brain structure AND explain its function — naming alone loses points
Know the dopamine connection to both Parkinson's (too little) and schizophrenia (too much)
Broca's = production (can't speak); Wernicke's = comprehension (can't understand) — this distinction is heavily tested

units.name: Cognition
key_topics: Classical conditioning: UCS, UCR, CS, CR, acquisition, extinction, spontaneous recovery, generalization, discrimination
Operant conditioning: reinforcement/punishment types, schedules (FR, VR, FI, VI), Skinner
Observational learning: Bandura's Bobo doll study, social learning theory
Memory models: Atkinson-Shiffrin multi-store, Baddeley's working memory
Levels of processing (structural, phonemic, semantic) and long-term potentiation
Long-term memory types: explicit (episodic, semantic) vs. implicit (procedural)
Forgetting: decay, proactive/retroactive interference, encoding failure, misinformation effect (Loftus)
Heuristics (availability, representativeness), biases (confirmation, anchoring, framing), mental set, functional fixedness
Perception: Gestalt principles, depth cues (monocular/binocular), top-down vs. bottom-up processing
Language development sequence and linguistic relativity (Sapir-Whorf hypothesis)
learning_objectives: Explain how classical and operant conditioning shape behavior
Compare memory models and describe how encoding, storage, and retrieval work
Identify causes of forgetting and memory distortion
Explain how heuristics and cognitive biases affect judgment and decision-making
Describe how perceptual principles and depth cues organize sensory information
key_researchers: Elizabeth Loftus — misinformation effect and eyewitness memory
George Miller — 7±2 chunks in short-term memory
Wolfgang Kohler — insight learning; Edward Tolman — latent learning and cognitive maps
common_question_types: Identify the conditioning term (CS, UCS, CR, etc.) from a described scenario
Distinguish reinforcement schedule type from a described pattern of rewards
Identify which memory system or forgetting mechanism applies to a described situation
Identify the heuristic or bias operating in a described judgment scenario
exam_tips: Variable ratio schedules produce the highest, most resistant-to-extinction response rates — a classic MCQ target
Distinguish proactive interference (old info disrupts new) from retroactive interference (new info disrupts old)
The availability heuristic is about ease of recall, not how recently something happened — students confuse these

units.name: Development and Learning
key_topics: Piaget's cognitive stages: sensorimotor (object permanence), preoperational (egocentrism, animism, conservation failure), concrete operational, formal operational
Vygotsky: zone of proximal development and scaffolding
Erikson's 8 psychosocial stages (Trust vs. Mistrust through Integrity vs. Despair)
Attachment: Harlow's contact comfort research, Ainsworth's strange situation and attachment styles
Parenting styles: authoritative, authoritarian, permissive (Baumrind)
Moral development (Kohlberg): preconventional, conventional, postconventional
Bronfenbrenner's ecological systems: microsystem, mesosystem, exosystem, macrosystem, chronosystem
Marcia's identity statuses: achievement, moratorium, foreclosure, diffusion
Prenatal development: teratogens and critical periods
Adolescent development: puberty, imaginary audience, personal fable, identity formation
learning_objectives: Describe Piaget's stages and identify the cognitive limitations of each
Explain attachment theory and the impact of early attachment on later development
Apply Erikson's psychosocial stages to described life situations
Compare Vygotsky's and Piaget's views on cognitive development
Explain how ecological context (Bronfenbrenner) shapes development
key_researchers: Erik Erikson — psychosocial stages across the lifespan
Harry Harlow — contact comfort and attachment in rhesus monkeys
Mary Ainsworth — attachment styles via the strange situation
Lawrence Kohlberg — stages of moral development
common_question_types: Identify the Piaget stage from a described child behavior
Match a described scenario to the correct Erikson stage and its core conflict
Identify the attachment style from a described parent-child interaction
Distinguish authoritative vs. authoritarian parenting from a described scenario
exam_tips: Preoperational children fail conservation tasks because of centration and irreversibility — know both reasons
Erikson's stage for adolescence is Identity vs. Role Confusion — distinct from Marcia's statuses, which describe how identity is formed
Harlow's monkeys preferred the cloth mother over the wire-feeding mother — evidence that attachment is based on comfort, not food

units.name: Social Psychology and Personality
key_topics: Attribution theory: fundamental attribution error, actor-observer bias, self-serving bias
Conformity (Asch line studies) and obedience (Milgram shock studies)
Normative vs. informational social influence
Group dynamics: groupthink, social loafing, social facilitation, deindividuation
Bystander effect and diffusion of responsibility
Cognitive dissonance (Festinger) and persuasion routes (elaboration likelihood model)
Prejudice, stereotypes, in-group bias, out-group homogeneity, just-world phenomenon
Freudian psychoanalytic theory: id, ego, superego, defense mechanisms, unconscious
Humanistic personality: Maslow's hierarchy of needs, Rogers' unconditional positive regard
Trait theory: Big Five (OCEAN) and personality assessment (MMPI, projective tests)
Emotion theories: James-Lange, Cannon-Bard, Schachter-Singer two-factor
Motivation theories: drive reduction, Yerkes-Dodson law, self-determination theory
learning_objectives: Explain how situational and dispositional factors are used to explain behavior
Describe how social influence processes affect individual behavior
Compare major personality theories and their assessment methods
Explain theories of emotion and how cognitive appraisal influences emotional experience
Identify sources of prejudice and strategies for reducing it
key_researchers: Sigmund Freud — psychoanalytic theory and defense mechanisms
Albert Bandura — reciprocal determinism and self-efficacy
Stanley Schachter — two-factor (cognitive arousal) theory of emotion
William James — James-Lange theory of emotion
common_question_types: Identify the attribution error or bias in a described scenario
Identify the social influence concept (conformity, obedience, compliance technique) operating in a scenario
Match a personality theory or defense mechanism to a described behavior
Identify the emotion theory that best explains a described sequence of events
exam_tips: Fundamental attribution error = overestimating disposition, underestimating situation for OTHERS' behavior
Two-factor theory requires both physiological arousal AND a cognitive label — the bridge study is the classic example

Defense mechanisms: know repression (unconscious blocking), projection (attributing to others), rationalization (logical excuses), and reaction formation (acting opposite) — these four appear most on the exam

units.name: Mental and Physical Health
key_topics: DSM-5 diagnostic framework, biopsychosocial model, diathesis-stress model
Schizophrenia: positive symptoms (hallucinations, delusions, disorganized speech/behavior) vs. negative symptoms (flat affect, alogia, avolition); dopamine hypothesis
Depressive disorders: major depressive disorder, persistent depressive disorder
Bipolar disorders: Bipolar I (full mania) vs. Bipolar II (hypomania)
Anxiety disorders: specific phobia, panic disorder, social anxiety disorder, GAD, agoraphobia
OCD, PTSD (flashbacks, hypervigilance, emotional numbing), dissociative disorders
Eating disorders: anorexia nervosa, bulimia nervosa
Psychotherapies: psychodynamic, humanistic (person-centered), CBT (cognitive triad, Beck), REBT (Ellis), DBT
Behavioral therapies: systematic desensitization, exposure therapy, aversion therapy, token economies
Biomedical treatments: antidepressants (SSRIs), antipsychotics, lithium, ECT, TMS
Stress and health: general adaptation syndrome (alarm, resistance, exhaustion), coping strategies, burnout
learning_objectives: Classify psychological disorders using DSM-5 categories and the biopsychosocial model
Describe the symptoms, causes, and treatments for major psychological disorders
Compare psychotherapy approaches and explain the mechanism of each
Explain biomedical treatments and their applications and side effects
Describe how stress affects physical and mental health via the GAS model
key_researchers: Aaron Beck — cognitive triad and CBT
Albert Ellis — Rational Emotive Behavior Therapy (REBT)
David Rosenhan — pseudo-patient study (challenges of psychiatric diagnosis)
common_question_types: Identify the psychological disorder from a described case vignette
Match a described therapy technique to the correct therapeutic approach
Identify the phase of the General Adaptation Syndrome from a described stress response
Distinguish positive vs. negative symptoms of schizophrenia in a described case
exam_tips: Positive symptoms of schizophrenia = things added (hallucinations, delusions); negative symptoms = things removed (flat affect, social withdrawal)
Systematic desensitization pairs relaxation with a fear hierarchy — distinct from flooding (immediate full exposure)
The diathesis-stress model explains why not everyone with a genetic predisposition develops a disorder — environment triggers it
